event_blocks.summary: A dramatic orchestral score underscores a daytime cityscape of a busy New York intersection, marked with '2009,' as a narrator references the '2008 housing crisis.' The scene shifts to a nighttime aerial view of Manhattan, highlighting illuminated streets and skyscrapers, including an iconic golden-tipped building, before focusing on the darkened glass facade of the 'J.P. Morgan Chase' building. The narrator explains how 'J.P. Morgan Chase' became the largest bank in America by navigating the 'once-in-a-lifetime financial storm.' In a dimly lit auditorium, a man identified as 'JAMIE DIMON' speaks and gestures to an audience. The orchestral music swells as a framed portrait of Dimon is shown, followed by another close-up of his face. A blue curtain transition reveals the glowing blue 'FINAiUS' logo with an ethereal sound effect and a female voice pronouncing 'Finius.' The music changes to an upbeat, percussive track with ambient city sounds, accompanying a bright, sunny day in Jackson Heights, NY, featuring pedestrians, cars, and a 'TACO' shop. The video concludes with a framed portrait of a younger Jamie Dimon, displayed in a gallery setting, as the narrator states, 'Jamie Dimon is born into the finance business.'

event_blocks.summary: Archival footage depicts a Titanic-like ocean liner navigating icy waters with tugboats as a narrator begins, stating, "In 1921, his grandfather came to New York with nothing," transitioning to a bustling 1920s/1930s city street scene with pedestrians and vintage automobiles while detailing the grandfather's rise as a banker and then stockbroker. A modern interior reveals a framed formal couple photograph as the narrator explains, "By the time Jamie's born, his father Theodore Diamond also has become a wealthy stockbroker." A man in a dark suit speaking animatedly appears, with the text 'women's libbers' briefly shown, as he recounts, "Well, you know, my parents were real moralists, so you they, you know, you do the right thing, you only do the right thing, you treat people everyone equally and fairly. My mom was one of the earliest women's libbers around after reading, uh, you know, some of those books and stuff like that." Grainy vintage home video footage of children playing near a wooden cabin and a lake follows, with the narrator describing, "Diamond's childhood is almost too perfect, attending private schools and spending summers in the mountains in Colorado." Framed black-and-white photographs of young boys, one with long hair, are displayed on a wall, as the narrator comments, "His life has been the envy of other children of the same age. But to the young Jamie, it isn't enough." The video concludes with Rocky Xu speaking expressively in a dimly lit room with a monitor visible, as the narrator states, "From day one, Jamie has an unwavering ambition. He wants to make a fortune, more than that of his father and grandfather." Rocky Xu then explains, "He recognized early on that to succeed achieving this goal, he should be in the finance industry." A dramatic and reflective instrumental score plays throughout.

event_blocks.summary: Despite graduating fourth in his class and facing rejection from his dream school, Brown University, Jamie Dimon attended Tufts University, where he was also rejected by Princeton University during his freshman year. Driven by a belief in his own caliber and a desire to prove his worthiness, Dimon resolved to become the top student at Tufts, a goal Rocky Xu describes as stemming from Dimon's supreme confidence, which sometimes appeared cocky. Dimon subsequently graduated summa cum laude from Tufts and was accepted into Harvard Business School, achieving his goal and validating his self-worth. At Harvard, Dimon, described by Xu as non-conformist for wearing jeans and leather jackets and being the only Democrat in a Republican-dominated class, graduated as a Baker Scholar, an honor for the top 5% of his class. Flooded with job offers, he turned them all down to work for Sandy Weill, a legendary financier, before a new voice began speaking about his father being a broker.

A close-up of the young man’s face appears, followed by a shot of a desk with the 'Abraham Lincoln' book, the Tufts ID card, and a handwritten document. The scene then shifts to Rocky Xu, a man in a white shirt, speaking expressively with hand gestures in a dimly lit room, as the narrator concludes that "Jamie Diamond feels he's entitled to go to the best school in America" and "decides the best way to prove he's worthy of greatness is by becoming the number one student at Tufts." Finally, Rocky Xu's voice takes over, describing how "Jamie Diamond always seemed to have this supreme confidence and sometimes comes off as cocky," as the final shot shows a framed photograph of the young man in a graduation gown, holding a paper, displayed on a wall, with Xu concluding, "His plan worked. event_blocks.summary: A man in a dark suit and striped tie, speaking animatedly before a 'COUNCIL ON FOREIGN RELATIONS' backdrop, recounts a past job offer where his Harvard Business School education was a factor, and the pitch involved experiencing a large company's operations. The scene then focuses on the book 'The REAL DEAL: MY LIFE in BUSINESS and PHILANTHROPY' by Sandy Weill, alongside his credit cards, as an upbeat score begins and a narrator introduces him as an accomplished dealmaker who influenced brands like Primerica, Solomon Brothers, and Citibank. The narrative shifts to a sunny Jackson Heights, NY street scene, noting Weill's birth in Brooklyn's Bensonhurst Jewish neighborhood. A montage of photographs depicts a younger Weill in various settings, illustrating his ambition to build his own company and utilize others' capital rather than a traditional nine-to-five job. A Wall Street Journal front page from September 5, 1997, announcing 'American Express to Acquire Shearson!', visually supports the account of Weill selling his firm Shearson to American Express for $900 million in 1986 and becoming its president. A color photograph of a man laughing with others accompanies the mention of Weill hiring Jamie Dimon as his personal assistant. The video concludes with a hand holding a New York Times article titled 'Deals in the Making', featuring a photo of two men shaking hands and the text 'Steve Wynn, RESORT & CASINO DEVELOPER', as a narrator's voice fades out with the phrase 'Like most young people who get breaks...', which is then echoed by a second male voice.

A Wall Street Journal front page from September 5, 1997, headlined 'American Express to Acquire Shearson!', visually reinforces the narrator's account: "By 1986, he sold his brokerage firm Shearson to American Express for $900 million. event_blocks.summary: The narrative begins with a close-up of a man in a dark suit and patterned tie, smiling in a dimly lit setting, as a dramatic score commences and a narrator introduces high-stakes deal-making worth hundreds of millions. The scene shifts to Jamie Dimon, a man with graying hair in a dark suit, against a city skyline, with the narrator noting his early perceived success at 28, but warning of a fall. A whoosh sound accompanies a transition to a nighttime aerial view of a suspension bridge, establishing a New York City atmosphere. The narrator then shifts to the 1980s, describing it as a decade of deal-making under President Reagan, characterized by tax cuts and easy borrowing, leading to mergers and acquisitions, with a second voice emphasizing "Only good deals." Visuals include a crowded indoor event with an American flag, a man in a dark suit and red tie speaking earnestly, and a dark, rainy urban street at night. Inside, a man in a blue suit smokes a cigar. A blurred abstract shot transitions to the narrator explaining that after working for the president of American Express, Jamie Dimon's career "skyrockets." Close-ups show a man with dark, wavy hair looking intently, followed by a group of men in suits in a boardroom discussion. The narrator concludes that Dimon knows if he helps his mentor, American Express will eventually be his. Rocky Xu, in a white button-up, narrates the corporate history of American Express, mentioning Sandy Weill's desire to sell the Fireman's Fund to Warren Buffett and his and Dimon's work on the deal, which the board vetoed. A close-up of an older man with gray hair and glasses, in a dark suit and red patterned tie, appears before returning to Rocky Xu, who states Sandy Weill quit American Express in anger but encouraged Dimon to stay. A close-up of a man with graying hair and a mustache in a dark suit is shown against a blue background. A framed photograph of a younger Jamie Dimon in a graduation gown is placed next to a book. An older man with graying hair, in a dark suit and red-and-blue striped tie, stands at a podium with a 'COUNCIL ON FOREIGN RELATIONS' backdrop, speaking about his decision to leave in 1985 due to management style differences, noting American Express stock tripled during his tenure. An exterior shot of the American Express Tower is shown. Rocky Xu's voice returns, stating the 29-year-old Dimon also quit. The older man at the podium concludes by describing Jamie Dimon as a 'very loyal guy.' The video opens with a man in a dark suit and red-and-blue striped tie, seated at a 'COUNCIL ON FOREIGN RELATIONS' backdrop, recounting an anecdote about "Jamie" being voted most likely to succeed. The scene transitions to a dimly lit, shadowy house interior. A serious male voice describes an "incredible gamble" and the need for a new plan as suspenseful music builds, accompanying a close-up of Jamie Dimon against a city skyline. The narrator details Sandy Weill's determination to make a comeback with Dimon, despite being seen as "on the way out" at 58, with no Wall Street firms wanting to hire him. This unfolds over visuals of a grand 'FOUR SEASONS' building facade, black-and-white close-ups of men in suits, and a man in a pinstripe suit with an 'Audio-Link' headset. The narrator explains Sandy Weill and Dimon are forced to look elsewhere for deals, aiming to take over smaller companies. Final scenes focus on a desk with financial documents, including a 'UNITED STATES SECURITIES AND EXCHANGE COMMISSION FORM 10-K' for 'Commercial Credit, Inc.' from 1988 and an 'ANNUAL REPORT' for the same company, under dim lighting.

raw_segments: The video opens with a close-up of a man in a dark suit and patterned tie, smiling broadly in a dimly lit setting, as a dramatic, suspenseful score begins. A narrator introduces the theme of high-stakes deal-making, mentioning "negotiating deals on behalf of while, worth hundreds of millions of dollars." The scene transitions to a close-up of Jamie Dimon, a man with wavy, graying hair in a dark suit and tie, standing against a city skyline, as the narrator states that "at the age of 28, Jamie Dimon already feels he's at the top of the finance game," but warns that "fate will bring him down to rock bottom." A whoosh sound accompanies a transition to a nighttime aerial view of a large suspension bridge, likely the Brooklyn Bridge, illuminated and bustling with traffic, establishing a New York City atmosphere. The narrator then shifts to the 1980s, describing it as "a decade of deal-making" under President Ronald Reagan, who "prioritized helping corporate America, cutting taxes and making it easy for them to borrow money," leading to "mergers and acquisitions, or simply, deals." A second, deeper male voice interjects, emphatically stating, "Only good deals." The visuals continue with a crowded indoor event featuring an American flag and clapping audience, followed by a man in a dark suit and red tie speaking earnestly. The scene then cuts to a dark, rainy urban street at night with a black car and pedestrians, creating a moody feel. Inside a modern setting, a man in a blue suit smokes a cigar, exuding authority. A blurred, fast-moving blue and black abstract shot serves as a transition. The narrator resumes, explaining that "after landing a job working directly for the president of American Express, Jamie Dimon's career skyrockets." A close-up of a man with dark, wavy hair looking intently is shown, followed by a group of men in suits in a serious boardroom discussion. The narrator concludes by noting that "Jamie Dimon knows, if he can help his mentor accomplish that, someday, American Express will also be his."

The video opens with Rocky Xu, a man in a white button-up shirt, speaking expressively and gesturing in a dimly lit room, narrating a corporate history. He explains in a clear voice how American Express, with its Fireman's Fund, saw Sandy Weill wanting to sell it to Warren Buffett, and how he and Jamie Dimon worked tirelessly on the deal, only for the board to veto it. A close-up of an older man with gray hair and glasses, wearing a dark suit and a red patterned tie, looks seriously at the camera before the scene returns to Rocky Xu, who continues his commentary, stating Sandy Weill quit American Express in anger but encouraged Dimon to stay. Next, a close-up of a man with graying hair and a mustache in a dark suit appears against a blue background, his expression intense. The narrative then shifts to a framed photograph of a younger Jamie Dimon, smiling in a graduation gown, placed next to a red book titled 'WHO'S AFRAID OF IBM?'. An older man with graying hair, wearing a dark suit and a red-and-blue striped tie, is then seen standing at a podium with a 'COUNCIL ON FOREIGN RELATIONS' backdrop, speaking in a distinct, somewhat gravelly voice. He recounts his decision to leave in 1985 due to a difference in management style, noting that American Express stock tripled during the four years he was there. An exterior shot of the American Express Tower, a grand stone building with classical architecture, arched windows, and columns, its name clearly visible above the glass entrance, is shown. The man at the podium continues his speech, followed by a close-up of another man in a dark suit and red tie looking serious. Rocky Xu's voice returns, stating the 29-year-old Dimon also quit his job. The older man at the podium concludes, describing Jamie Dimon as a 'very loyal guy' who worked for him.

The video opens with a man in a dark suit and red-and-blue striped tie, seated in front of a 'COUNCIL ON FOREIGN RELATIONS' backdrop, gesturing as he speaks. He recounts a personal anecdote, mentioning "Jamie" and how he was voted most likely to succeed in business school, reflecting on not having to work another day in his life. The scene then transitions to a dimly lit, shadowy house interior where a figure moves through a doorway, creating a mysterious atmosphere. A serious, narrative male voice begins to speak, describing an "incredible gamble" that will pay off, but for now, a new plan is needed. Suspenseful, building orchestral music starts, accompanying a close-up of Jamie Dimon against a city skyline. The narrator continues, detailing how "the unyielding Sandy Weill is determined to make a comeback, with Dimon on his side," despite being seen by peers as "on the way out" at 58, with no Wall Street firms wanting to hire him—a mistake they will regret. This narrative unfolds over visuals of a grand, ornate 'FOUR SEASONS' building facade illuminated at night, followed by a series of black-and-white close-ups of serious men in suits, and a man in a pinstripe suit with an 'Audio-Link' headset. The narrator explains that "Sandy Weill and Dimon are forced to look elsewhere for deals," aiming to take over smaller companies to leverage them for bigger ones. The final scenes focus on a desk with financial documents, including a 'UNITED STATES SECURITIES AND EXCHANGE COMMISSION FORM 10-K' for 'Commercial Credit, Inc.' from 1988 and an 'ANNUAL REPORT' for the same company, alongside glasses, a pen, and a coffee cup, all under dim, moody lighting, emphasizing the corporate and financial scrutiny central to the ongoing narration.

event_blocks.summary: The narrative begins with an aerial view of Baltimore's skyline, transitioning to a close-up of the 'Commercial Credit Building,' identified by Rocky Xu as a high-interest loan company targeting middle and lower-class consumers, which he describes as a 'loan shark operation.' A mechanical whirring sound accompanies a visual of a 'CONTROL DATA | 6600' computer terminal, as Rocky Xu reveals Commercial Credit's parent company, 'Control Data,' a computer disk drive maker, made a 'bizarre acquisition doomed to fail.' A 'New York Times' article titled 'An Era Ends at Control Data' is shown, illustrating the parent company's struggles, leading to its financial trouble by 1985 and readiness to sell its subsidiary. An older man is then seen reading a 'Form 10-K' document for 'Commercial Credit, Inc.' from 1988, as Rocky Xu explains that Sandy Weill instructed Jamie Dimon to analyze the firm for a takeover. Dimon's 'shocking finding' is revealed as Rocky Xu states Commercial Credit was a 'crappy company with just 4% return on equity,' a figure displayed as '4% ROE' text overlay, contrasting with the 15% return of similar companies, while upbeat, slightly dramatic music plays throughout.

raw_segments: The video opens with a sweeping aerial view of Baltimore’s skyline, showcasing modern buildings and a harbor filled with boats, with the word 'BALTIMORE' prominently displayed. Upbeat, slightly dramatic background music begins as a male voice, Rocky Xu, starts narrating, explaining the search for a company from an "unlikely place." The scene transitions to a close-up of a tall building labeled 'Commercial Credit.' Rocky Xu continues, identifying Commercial Credit as "a loan company in Baltimore, targeting middle and lower-class consumers," and describing it as "pretty much a loan shark operation" lending high-interest, short-term loans. A distinct mechanical whirring sound accompanies a cut to a vintage 'CONTROL DATA | 6600' computer terminal with two green circular screens, as Rocky Xu reveals that Commercial Credit's parent company is "a computer disk drive maker, called Control Data," which he characterizes as "a bizarre acquisition, doomed to fail from the get-go." The visuals then show a close-up of a 'New York Times' article titled 'An Era Ends at Control Data,' detailing the company's struggles. Rocky Xu explains that by 1985, Control Data was "in financial trouble" and ready to sell its child company for cash. An older man is then seen from behind, intently reading a 'Form 10-K' document for 'Commercial Credit, Inc.' from 1988. Rocky Xu continues, stating that "Sandy Weill instructs Jamie Dimon to analyze the firm for a potential takeover," and Dimon "comes back with a shocking finding." As Rocky Xu describes Jamie Dimon as someone who "thinks differently," the text '4% ROE' appears on screen, directly correlating with Rocky Xu's subsequent statement that "Commercial Credit was a crappy company with just 4% return on equity," contrasting it with similar companies having 15%. The music continues throughout, maintaining an analytical and historical tone.

event_blocks.summary: Rocky Xu, in a dimly lit room, explains Warren Buffett's investment philosophy shift from 'cigar butt companies' to 'quality companies,' influenced by Charlie Munger, as a copy of 'The Snowball: Warren Buffett and the Business of Life' and a Coca-Cola can are shown. Warren Buffett, in an interview, reflects on lessons from Ben Graham and the difficulties of scaling 'scraunchy and unpleasant' businesses involving layoffs. Rocky Xu narrates that Buffett rejected an offer, making a comeback seem impossible, and outlines three takeover methods: a wealthy co-investor like Buffett (which failed), a leverage buyout (LBO), and a public offering (IPO). The narrative shifts to urban imagery and a warning about the risks of heavy debt from buyouts, then focuses on the IPO challenge. Sandy Weill is shown intensely focused at his desk, with a newspaper clipping detailing his appointment; he invested most of his fortune and offered 80% to the public. Jamie Dimon, despite being in his early 30s, invested $425,000, as indicated by a '$425K' overlay. A black screen announces 'THE IPO WAS A SUCCESS,' followed by stock trading scenes with an '$850M' overlay, signifying the successful $850 million raised. The segment concludes with two smiling men in suits, as Rocky Xu reveals Sandy Weill appointed Dimon as CFO and gave him a substantial number of shares, with a consistent, slightly dramatic background music underscoring the events.

He narrates how Warren Buffett was approached for investment, explaining Buffett's shift from 'cigar butt companies' to 'quality companies,' influenced by Charlie Munger. This commentary plays over a close-up of 'The Snowball: Warren Buffett and the Business of Life' book by Alice Schroeder, placed beside a red Coca-Cola can. The scene then cuts to Warren Buffett, an older man with white hair and glasses, wearing a dark suit and red tie, seated and speaking during an interview. Buffett is later seen in a different setting, wearing a striped tie and a gold watch, interacting with a smiling man in a blue suit. Rocky Xu's voice returns, stating Buffett rejected the offer, making an 'epic comeback' seem 'impossible.' Rocky continues, explaining 'three possible ways to take over this company,' including a wealthy co-investor like Buffett (which failed), and as he mentions the second way, a 'leverage buyout,' the large white text 'LBO' appears on screen. A subtle, dramatic background music underscores the entire informative and analytical narrative.

The segment opens with a gritty urban scene featuring a weathered stone wall, a poster declaring 'YOU CAN'T HANDLE LEVERAGE!!', a red British postbox with an orange cone, and parked bicycles, as a male narrator discusses the risks of taking over a company by borrowing heavily, leading to a heavy debt burden and potential bankruptcy. He then introduces a third option: a public offering by selling ownership to the public, with the challenge of convincing investors. This transitions to Rocky Xu, in a white button-up shirt, speaking expressively in a dimly lit room with a monitor in the background, as the word 'IPO' appears, visually reinforcing the narrator's explanation of public offerings. A newspaper clipping titled 'Control Data Unit Gets Weill' details his appointment, while the narrator explains that Jamie Dimon sees commercial credit as an undervalued company, and Weill decided to put the majority of his own fortune, buying 10% of the company, and offering 80% to the public. event_blocks.summary: The narrative begins with a close-up of two smiling men in suits, one with graying hair and a mustache, as a male voice discusses taking a company public to secure an investment-grade credit rating. This transitions to an older man in academic regalia speaking at a podium, followed by an exterior shot of the 'Commercial Credit' building amidst ambient city sounds and a building background score. Upon taking over Commercial Credit, costs were ruthlessly cut, including laying off 10% of employees. Two serious men are seen in an office as the company's success is detailed: $100 million in profit and an 18% return on equity, visually reinforced by a black-and-white courtroom scene overlaid with these figures. A close-up of the 'Standard & Poor's' sign appears, also with the '$100M' text, as credit agencies increased the company's rating, allowing them to borrow an additional $100 million. A contemplative man outdoors at night and a dramatically lit older man with a serious expression are shown as the narrator states that with this money, they could pursue 'deals.' The background music intensifies dramatically as the narrator reveals that before they could act, the company's stock suddenly collapsed by 50%, culminating in a black screen displaying 'October 19, 1987,' with the narrator confirming, 'That day is October 19th...' The segment then shifts to a vintage television set displaying chaotic scenes from a stock exchange floor, where traders in colorful jackets are seen in a frenzied state, pushing and shouting, as a male narrator describes the "1987 panic, crash, freefall, meltdown," noting how people "just put their hands in their pockets and stared in disbelief." The screen transitions to a digital display showing the S&P 500 Index with a sharp decline, highlighting a dramatic market drop. Alan Greenspan, identified as the 'Former Chairman, Federal Reserve,' speaks with a serious expression in warm, dim lighting, stating that "every crisis of this type, whether it's 2008 or 1873, has got the same roots, namely that it was unanticipated." The video returns to the vintage TV, now showing a close-up of a trader in a red jacket yelling, reinforcing the panic. A framed photograph of two smiling men in suits is shown, followed by an exterior shot of the grand 'Commercial Credit' building with green columns. A digital display beside its entrance shows 'Fortress Balance Sheet' with '1987' and images of the two men from the photograph, as the narrator explains that "while many of the biggest companies in the country struggled to survive, Weill and Diamond see an opportunity," believing that "with a strong balance sheet, Commercial Credit simply fitter than anybody else." The scene transitions to a close-up of a New York Times article dated September 20, 1992, titled 'Sandy Weill, Takeover Prince, Goes on the Prowl Again,' detailing his financial ambitions and acquisitions, with a black-and-white photo of him at his desk. The narrator continues, describing how "they start buying back their shares cheaply and taking over struggling firms next to nothing," becoming "the most prolific dealmakers" and even managing "to devour a bigger company, an insurance company called Primerica, with Jamie Diamond as the architect behind the deal."

event_blocks.summary: On October 8th, the official merger date, Chairman and Co-CEOs John Reed and Sandy Weill, smiling broadly before a large 'CITIGROUP' logo, celebrated the creation of "the biggest financial conglomerate in America" amidst dramatic background music. The scene quickly shifted to a chaotic press scrum of photographers and reporters, followed by an aerial view of the distinctive Citigroup Center skyscraper against the New York City skyline and Central Park. Sandy Weill, with a wide, confident smile, was declared the "king of Wall Street," while Jamie Dimon, with a reserved expression, was introduced as president, managing investment funds and described as "only one step away" from becoming the most powerful man in finance. event_blocks.summary: Sanford Weill, former Citigroup Chairman & CEO, recounted a past professional conflict where his subordinate felt ready to run the company, but Weill wasn't ready to retire, leading to non-cooperation. The narrative then shifted to Jamie Dimon, described as no stranger to failure, who, over years of corporate conquest with Sandy Weill, built a strong reputation as an effective leader, though he believed he was robbed of his right to become Citigroup's CEO. The segment highlighted Dimon's ambition to build an empire bigger than Citigroup, using a business as a launching pad, with a perfect opportunity landing at his doorstep by 1999. Concurrently, Bank One, a badly run Chicago-based financial conglomerate with a poorly rated credit card due to customer service and hidden fees, was seeking a 'wartime CEO' after firing its CEO, John 'Fly-By McCoy,' known for lavish vacations amidst poor company earnings. Jamie Dimon was identified as the only man capable of turning the bank around. Dimon reportedly went 'all in,' putting his fortune, over $50 million, in Bank One stocks, which then rose 30%. Nassim Nicholas Taleb's 'skin in the game' philosophy was presented, emphasizing that individuals should face harm if they put others at risk. Despite this, 'Diamond' (Dimon) gambled his future on Bank One, potentially underestimating its poor state. A man in a dark suit and purple tie described Bank One as tougher than JP Morgan and failing, necessitating layoffs to fix the company. Dimon, seen walking toward the 'BANK ONE' building and later in a car, planned to sell underperformed businesses, cut executive compensation, and fire 12,000 employees to cut costs and reshape the company.

Rocky Xu, a man in a light-colored shirt, then takes over the narration in a dimly lit room, explaining that “Bank One is a badly run financial conglomerate based in Chicago,” whose credit card was “rated the worst in the industry” due to poor customer service and hidden fees. The visual shifts to a black-and-white magazine spread titled 'Meet ‘Fly-By McCoy’', featuring a smiling man in a suit, as Rocky Xu identifies him as Bank One CEO John McCoy, “nicknamed 'Fly-By McCoy',” who was known for “lavish vacations while the company has poor earnings.” Rocky Xu continues, stating the board “fires McCoy” and is “urgently looking for a wartime CEO” to turn the bank around, concluding that “there’s only one man capable of turning the bank around: Jamie Dimon.” The scene then shows a man with graying hair in a dark sweater, holding a glass against a city backdrop, as the background music becomes more dramatic and suspenseful. This man then speaks, stating that “To show he means business, Jamie Dimon goes all in, virtually putting...” before the audio cuts off, as the video returns to an aerial cityscape and finally a close-up of a man in a suit smiling warmly.

The video opens with a man in a dark suit and patterned tie, smiling confidently as large white text '$50M' appears over him, while a calm, informative narrator reveals he has "all of his fortune in Bank One stocks, worth over $50 million." The scene cuts to a black screen with white text stating 'The stock goes up 30%,' indicating a positive market movement. Next, Nassim Nicholas Taleb, a man with a white beard and glasses, wearing a dark blazer, stands in front of a whiteboard with a graph, gesturing as he speaks in a clear, authoritative voice, explaining his "skin in the game" rule: "nobody should put others at risk without having harm to himself," emphasizing that if you lose money for clients, "you should be exposed to the same risk." A book cover titled 'SKIN IN THE GAME: Hidden Asymmetries in Daily Life' by Nassim Nicholas Taleb is shown, linking the lecture to the book’s themes. The narrator then introduces a figure named "Diamond" gambling his future on Bank One, which he may have "underestimated just how bad the company is." A man in a dark suit and purple tie, speaking animatedly against a red-lit background with a slightly gravelly voice, describes how "Bank One was much tougher than JP Morgan," "failing," with "stocks down," and the need to "fix a company" by potentially laying off "a lot of people." The video transitions to an exterior shot of a classical-style building labeled 'BANK ONE,' with a man in a white shirt walking toward it, implying a purposeful visit by "Diamond." Finally, the same man in the dark suit and purple tie is seen in the back of a dark car, adjusting his tie and speaking, conveying a sense of urgency or contemplation as he travels through a cityscape, as the narrator details how "Diamond" will "sell underperformed businesses," "cut executive compensation," and "fire 12,000 employees to cut costs" to beat the company into shape.

event_blocks.summary: Jamie Dimon, seated in a black leather chair against a muted green and gray wall, passionately recounts eliminating employee benefits such as cars, club memberships, 401K matches, and financial planning, stating these were entitlements and that he and others were to blame for the situation, urging those in good shape to lead in cutting their own benefits first. The video then displays text overlays of '$4.4B' and '-$511M' with a black-and-white filter. Rocky Xu explains that Dimon incurred $4.4 billion in write-downs and loan loss provisions in 2000, resulting in a $511 million loss for the year. Xu, speaking animatedly in a dimly lit room with a monitor, further analyzes that Wall Street approves of Dimon's actions, causing the stock to rise because they perceive someone willing to do what is necessary, and that Dimon's sole objective is building a fortress balance sheet. A book titled 'A FORTRESS FOR BANKING' by Jamie Dimon is shown on a desk, as Xu continues that a true fortress balance sheet is essential. The final scene shows Frank Bisignano, CEO of Fiserv, standing in a dark suit and yellow tie, gesturing while speaking against a dark backdrop.

raw_segments: The video opens with a close-up of Jamie Dimon, a man with graying hair in a dark suit and red-and-blue striped tie, seated in a black leather chair against a muted green and gray wall. event_blocks.summary: A male lecturer in a dark suit and yellow tie explained the importance of having 'assets that really have value' and being 'not leveraged' for 'shock absorption,' a concept that contrasted sharply with the subsequent nighttime exterior of the Bank One building, overlaid with '$2.6B', as a narrator announced Bank One's astounding $2.6 billion profit a year later, crediting Jamie Dimon with turning the company around from collapse and being 'ready for the hunt.' This was visually underscored by Jamie Dimon, in a dark suit and purple tie, looking contemplative in a moving car, adjusting his tie while a car screen showed a news broadcast with the headline 'Is Dimon Ready to Hunt?' and a 'BREAKING NEWS' banner, before receiving a call from 'Bill Harrison' on a BlackBerry. The scene then shifted to a daytime cityscape with tall modern buildings and a historic stone structure, a street-level crowd holding up phones, and an aerial view of a dense urban skyline, all set against slightly ominous music as the narrator detailed the economic downturns of the late 1990s and early 2000s, including Federal Reserve interest rate hikes, the dot-com burst, and the 9/11 attack's impact on the stock market. The narrative then returned to an aerial view of the dense urban skyline and the illuminated 'Bank One' sign at night, with the narrator highlighting that by the fourth quarter of 2003, Bank One achieved a 59% return under Jamie Dimon, three times the market's rebound. Jamie Dimon, in a dark suit and blue tie, was shown smiling and pointing confidently at the camera with a '+59%' graphic overlay, reinforcing his reputation as a 'cut-throat turn-around guy' patiently plotting his comeback after an exile from City Group. He was later seen in a dark overcoat walking purposefully from the Bank One building at night, his composed expression reflecting his strategic nature, as suspenseful, ominous music with a ticking sound built towards a close-up of a 'J.P. MORGAN CHASE & CO.' sign and a towering glass skyscraper. The narrator then introduced J.P. Morgan, founded by 'J. Pierpont Morgan,' with visuals including a framed black-and-white portrait of a man with a mustache and a bookshelf displaying 'THE HOUSE OF MORGAN' by Ron Chernow, connecting modern finance to its foundational figures.

event_blocks.summary: Alan Greenspan, identified as 'Former Chairman, Federal Reserve,' described someone as "really an extraordinary character," "steely-eyed, determined, very smart," and possessing the characteristic, "I will take charge," accompanied by a close-up of a man with a prominent mustache and a black-and-white archival image of a man in a top hat shouting at a crowd, all underscored by dramatic background music. A narrator stated that by 2003, "JP Morgan Chase has become a sleeping giant," as a low-angle shot of the 'JPMORGAN CHASE & CO.' sign on a sleek building was shown. A female news reporter in a red jacket reported live on the "collapse of the deal between Enron and Dynegy," leaving "Enron employees stunned" and "wondering if they'll be getting their next paycheck." The narrator explained that "The Enron scandal cost JP Morgan $135 million in fines and a negative public image," as a blurred SEC Settlement Document detailing 'J.P. Morgan Chase Agrees to Pay $135 Million to Settle SEC Allegations' was displayed. The narrator continued that "The current CEO, William Harrison, is at his retirement age," while a calendar with a man's photo was shown, adding that "to reverse the fortune of JP Morgan Chase, the company must find a man, just like its founder, J. Pierpont Morgan," as a framed black-and-white portrait of the historical financier was presented. The narrator concluded, "By the start of 2003, he finds such a man," coinciding with a Man in White Shirt and Blue Tie casually talking on a phone. Rocky Xu, in a light-colored shirt, spoke animatedly, explaining that Bank One and JP Morgan were looking to do deals, with JP Morgan able to use Bank One to strengthen its branch banking business, and that the deal's true attractiveness lay in acquiring Jamie Dimon as the successor to William Harrison, who made it clear Dimon would become CEO. The scene transitioned to a formal setting with two men in suits before a blue backdrop with the 'J.P. MORGAN CHASE & CO.' logo, while Rocky's narration continued over subtle, slightly suspenseful background music. Rocky Xu narrated how JP Morgan Chase merged with Bank One, and after a year, Jamie Dimon officially became CEO of JP Morgan Chase, then the second largest bank in America, second only to Citigroup, "but not for long." A close-up of a Newspaper Article (BANKING GIANT) by Landon Thomas Jr. (Jan 15, 2004) was shown, discussing James L. Dimon’s rise alongside a black-and-white photograph of a younger Portrait of Jamie Dimon (Younger) smiling subtly, as the background music faded out. Rocky Xu revealed that many people don't know JP Morgan is among the oldest and most established institutional collectors of blue-chip contemporary art, supported by a Digital Article (JP Morgan Art Collection) titled 'Inside legendary financier JP Morgan's $900 million personal art collection,' featuring an ornate, gilded library interior. The final shot was a dark, textured Portrait of a Man with Mustache emerging from shadow, likely J.P. Morgan, as Rocky Xu's narration concluded, mentioning this tradition was established by "the founder."

raw_segments: The video opens with a dramatic low-angle shot of towering glass and steel skyscrapers under a bright blue sky, emphasizing the imposing scale of the urban financial district. A deep, resonant sound effect transitions into a low, suspenseful drone as an unidentified male narrator begins to speak, setting the scene. The camera then cuts to a dimly lit street at night, where a man in a dark suit sits alone on a metal bench, appearing contemplative or distressed, with a chain-link fence and orange streetlight in the background. The narrator's voice continues, discussing JP Morgan's strategy of "cutting costs, reforming incentive structure, and most importantly, creating a fortress balance sheet," and how "Wall Street only cares about profit." The scene shifts to a daytime exterior of a modern building prominently displaying the 'Citi' logo, while the narrator explains the challenge to "beat City Group in the marketplace" and how "the market is getting irrationally exuberant," with "City Group" being "the lead dancer in the housing boom." The video then transitions to an interview setting with George Soros, identified as 'Chairman, Soros Fund Management,' seated in a well-lit office with large windows overlooking city buildings. With expressive hand gestures, Soros speaks with an older, distinct accent, stating that "Chuck Prince of Citibank famously said that we have to dance until the music stops. Actually, the music had stopped already when he said that." Following this, the video cuts to a dimly lit interview with a man in a black shirt, who speaks thoughtfully in a shadowy room with framed artwork in the background, though his voice is not heard in this segment. The sequence ends with a return to the cityscape, showing a low-angle view of sleek skyscrapers under a clear sky, reinforcing the theme of corporate power and urban grandeur, as the narrator concludes by mentioning "Diamond" needing to "watch from the sidelines." The suspenseful background music continues throughout.

event_blocks.summary: In 2007, as the American economy faced a deepening recession driven by a housing slump, Bear Stearns became the first bank to fail, a situation exacerbated by its record-breaking profits from subprime loan securitization, where hands were seen counting U.S. dollar bills and a diagram illustrated the complex flow of loan payments connecting mortgages to global investors. Rocky Xu explained how financial institutions shifted risk and how the government sought a financially sound company to bail out Bear Stearns, with J.P. Morgan Chase, under Jamie Dimon's leadership, being the only entity with minimal subprime exposure. Dimon, speaking from the J.P. Morgan Chase building and later in a CNBC interview, asserted the bank's strong capital and liquidity, stating the chance of a run on J.P. Morgan was 'basically zero' and their balance sheet a 'fortress.' He clarified that J.P. Morgan bought Bear Stearns, with the government's role being minimal, stating, 'We did the whole thing.' Archival footage showed Dimon walking outside a classical building, with a narrator describing him as a 'shrewd banker' offering '$2 a share' for Bear Stearns, whose stock had plummeted from $170 to $2.00 per share amidst news of a Wall Street crash and Federal Reserve action. Ultimately, Bear Stearns was sold to J.P. Morgan Chase for $10 per share, a deal that, while not immediately beneficial to J.P. Morgan's bottom line, certified its reputation as a sound bank and propelled Jamie Dimon to the top of the financial industry, as evidenced by books like 'Last Man Standing: The Ascent of Jamie Dimon and JPMorgan Chase' and 'The House of Dimon: How JPMorgan’s Jamie Dimon Rose to the Top of the Financial World.'

raw_segments: The segment opens with a man with graying hair in a black shirt, speaking thoughtfully in a dimly lit room, his calm voice narrating about financial figures like "Chuck Prince, who ran Citigroup to the ground." A somber, investigative background music begins as the scene transitions to an aerial view of a sprawling cityscape at dusk, overlaid with '2007,' while a serious female narrator introduces "the biggest financial story of 2007," the "housing slump." The camera pans across the city, highlighting bridges and illuminated buildings, before cutting to an overhead shot of a construction site, then to a close-up of an orange and gray tent. The initial male narrator's voice returns, explaining that "the American economy finds itself in a bigger recession" due to "the housing market," and that "the first bank to fail is Bear Stearns." Archival footage of a chaotic stock exchange floor, with traders in motion and monitors flashing, is accompanied by loud bells, shouting, and a distinct "ding," as on-screen ABC News text states 'Bear Stearns was under pressure' and 'Wall Street woke up to a bad dream.' An urgent male news reporter's voice confirms, "Wall Street woke up this morning only to face a bad dream," identifying "Investment giant Bear Stearns" as "now one of the most endangered." The exterior of the Bear Stearns building is shown, with text noting it was 'one of the most endangered' firms. The initial male narrator's voice continues, explaining how "Bear Stearns made a record-breaking profit through the business of subprime loan securitization," as hands count U.S. dollar bills. A diagram illustrating the flow of loan payments from home buyers to lenders, investment banks, and investors appears with on-screen text overlays like 'LOAN PAYMENTS' and 'INVESTORS,' while the narrator details how this "connects trillions of dollars in mortgages and other loans with investors all over the world." The segment concludes with Rocky Xu, a man in a light-colored shirt, speaking animatedly with expressive hand gestures in a dimly lit room, visually explaining the financial system, as the initial male narrator's voice-over concludes, "They thought by using financial derivatives, the risk was eliminated."

The segment opens with Rocky Xu, a man in a light-colored shirt, speaking animatedly in a dimly lit room with a monitor in the background, using expressive hand gestures. His clear, articulate male voice explains how financial institutions shifted or hid risk, and how the government stepped in when Bear Stearns was on the verge of collapse, seeking a fit financial company to bail it out, noting only one company on Wall Street had minimal exposure to subprime loans. A bright, wide aerial shot of the U.S. Capitol Building under clear skies follows, then a nighttime exterior of the J.P. Morgan Chase & Co. building. The scene shifts to a man in a dark suit and blue tie (Jamie Dimon) standing confidently in front of a large 'JPMORGAN' sign, whose deep, authoritative voice discusses having more capital and liquidity, stating the chance of a run on J.P. Morgan was 'basically zero' and calling their balance sheet a 'fortress.' After a brief visual of a panel discussion at The Aspen Institute, Rocky Xu returns, continuing in his analytical voice to explain that ever since Jamie Dimon took over J.P. Morgan Chase, he had been reducing their exposure to subprime mortgage loans, and that the federal government initially didn't grasp the situation's gravity, with the Republican president unwilling to nationalize a bank. The segment concludes with Jamie Dimon, wearing a dark suit and blue tie, speaking expressively in an interview setting with a CNBC logo visible, his confident voice clarifying that J.P. Morgan bought Bear Stearns and that the government's role was minimal, stating, 'We did the whole thing.'

The video opens with Jamie Dimon, a man with graying hair in a dark suit and blue tie, speaking expressively in a formal CNBC interview. His clear, confident voice explains, "we just reduced... reduced the balance sheet we were taking on. And we thought we're helping the system. We only did it because we asked to by Hank Paulson. And we thought if it went down, that could be a huge crisis for the globe." The scene transitions to archival footage of Dimon walking alongside another man outside a classical building, as a male narrator, accompanied by dramatic orchestral music, describes him as a "shrewd banker" offering "$2 a share." News footage of the Bear Stearns building appears, with ABC News overlays declaring 'DEVELOPING STORY: WALL STREET CRASH, FED ACTS TO STEM CRISIS,' and graphics showing its stock plummeting from $170 to $2.00 per share. An energetic news reporter's voice announces, "Bear Stearns, the Wall Street legend... is being sold to JP Morgan Chase for just $2 a share," noting that "last year Bear shares were selling for $170." The narrator clarifies that the initial offer was "too small" and the company was ultimately "sold to JPM at $10 per share." The camera pans across the Bear Stearns glass facade before cutting to the J.P. Morgan building at night, symbolizing the acquisition. The narrator explains that while the deal didn't benefit J.P. Morgan's bottom line, it "certified JP Morgan's reputation as a sound bank to do business with" and "sends Jamie Dimon to the very top of the financial industry." The sequence concludes with Dimon walking confidently through a bustling city street, followed by a close-up of two books, 'Last Man Standing: The Ascent of Jamie Dimon and JPMorgan Chase' and 'The House of Dimon: How JPMorgan’s Jamie Dimon Rose to the Top of the Financial World,' both featuring his image, underscoring his powerful rise. The narrator's voice continues, stating, "By the end of 2008, Jamie Dimon is a practical..." before the audio fades.

event_blocks.summary: The narrative begins by highlighting Jamie Dimon's resilience, with books like 'Last Man Standing' and 'The House of Dimon' underscoring his position as "the last man standing" and the ascendance of "The House of Morgan" to "The House of Dimon." Visuals shift from a sunlit New York City street to a nighttime J.P. Morgan building at East 47th St and Madison Ave, then a cityscape featuring One World Trade Center, overlaid with financial figures indicating J.P. Morgan Chase's strength during the 2008 recession, having absorbed $200 billion in money market management and increased commercial banking income by 27%. In contrast, a Citi building appears, followed by a black-and-white newspaper article from November 6, 2007, detailing the 'Ouster of Citigroup Chief' Charles O. Prince III, whose contemplative photo accompanies a dramatic musical shift signaling Citigroup's different trajectory. A color image of Prince is shown, then a man in a dark suit speaking at a podium with a 'group' sign, identified as the leader who mistakenly relied heavily on troubled mortgage-backed securities after Sandy Weill's retirement, leading to the board's decision to replace Prince with Vikram Pandit. The segment concludes somberly with a black screen and the narrator's statement that "it was already too late" and "the damage is done," as Sandy Weill watched the "storm unfold" from retirement. The video then shifts to Rocky Xu stating that "firing Jamie Dimon is perhaps the worst mistake of his career," followed by a framed photograph of two men in suits and Sanford Weill expressing regret in a CNBC studio, wishing he had been present instead of Chuck Prince. Rocky Xu describes the "complicated relationship like father and son" between Weill and Dimon, and Weill's secret pride in Dimon's achievements. Rocky Xu appears on screen, animatedly discussing Dimon's resilience as "the last man standing" on Wall Street after the 2008 recession, accompanied by upbeat, slightly dramatic instrumental music. Visuals of Jamie Dimon walking confidently through a city alley, a Fortune magazine article naming him the most admired CEO, and Dimon speaking at podiums with 'FORTUNE' and 'FAT' signs reinforce Rocky Xu's praise of Dimon as a "very principled person" who lives by his values and treats everyone equally.

event_blocks.summary: Jamie Dimon, characterized by his graying hair, mustache, and attire of a dark suit, white shirt, and bright pink tie, spoke assertively at a podium bearing a purple front panel with 'FAT' in white letters. Against a dark blue curtain backdrop, he gestured expressively while delivering a motivational message in a clear, assertive male voice, urging the audience to reject "bullshit," to "get up every day, do the best you can, tell the truth, fight every day," and proudly declared, "I put the jersey on at JP Morgan, every single day." Following this address, a blue curtain wipe transitioned to a black screen where a stylized blue 'FINAiUS' logo, distinguished by its modern, sleek design, a small blue dot above the 'i', and a glowing effect, was displayed for the remainder of the segment, underscored by dramatic, building instrumental music.
